transcript:
Here's what's actually happening: nothing is random in the moment-to-moment sense — that seeded generator decides, once, at the very start, exactly which cells will stay calm and which will detonate. Look at the thresholds in the setup: anything under 0.4 stays "stable," but push past 0.95 and a cell gets the "collapse" class, tied to that keyframe called "systematic destruction" — the one where a square scales down to one percent of itself and spins one thousand four hundred forty degrees before snapping back. It's not entropy. It's a predetermined fate assigned to every tile before the first frame even paints.

And notice the loop at the bottom, "maintain collapse" — the comment literally says "no objects allocated, the destruction is purely CSS-driven." The JavaScript isn't animating anything; it's just watching, frame after frame, doing nothing but confirming the collapse continues without it.

So what you're watching isn't chaos overtaking order — it's order so rigid it was scripted to look like chaos, forever, on a loop, back to zero every ten seconds.
